name: $codex_ent_aleoids_01_name;
description: This Aleoida Species has upright clumps of long serrated leaves, which can open up to expose a reproductive organ containing tiny round seeds.

name: $codex_ent_aleoids_02_name;
description: This Aleoida Species has an interleaved crown of mottled leaves that can grow to head height, with explosive seed pods emerging on long protruding stalks.

name: $codex_ent_aleoids_05_name;
description: This Aleoida Species has wide flat leaves on a heavy bark base and can reach huge sizes. It can sprout a dome-shaped reproductive organ at its peak.

name: $codex_ent_aleoids_04_name;
description: This Aleoida Species has a circle of upturned leaves marked with patterns, surrounding a bright fleshy pod with darker markings which matures in its centre.

name: $codex_ent_aleoids_03_name;
description: This Aleoida Species features long spiky leaves that can reach over two metres high surrounding a single reproductive organ on a long central stalk.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_06_name;
description: A Bacterial Species found in ammonia-based atmospheres that lives in sunlight. A colony’s appearance resembles an intricate maze.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_01_name;
description: These Bacteria thrive on sunlight in Carbon Dioxide atmospheres. They cause blanket coloration across a planetary surface.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_12_name;
description: A sunlight-converting Bacterial Species on worlds with atmospheres dominated by water and sulphur dioxide. Their colonies resemble a brain-shaped mass of smaller connected cells.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_04_name;
description: A Bacterial Species that converts energy from neon-based atmospheres, creating looping whirls of bright colour.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_10_name;
description: This Species of Bacteria thrives on atmospheric methane, appearing as a network of linked bubble patterns.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_08_name;
description: This Species of Bacteria can be found in nitrogen atmospheres and form a shapeless mass across the surface.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_02_name;
description: This Bacterial Species survives exclusively on atmospheric helium on Icy Bodies. They are distinguished by a radial pattern extending outward from the colony’s centre.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_11_name;
description: This Species of Bacteria converts geothermal heat from nitrogen- or ammonia-based volcanic sites into energy. They appear as long interlinked strands across the surface.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_03_name;
description: This Species of Bacteria thrives on the heat generated by carbon- or methane-based volcanic activity and appears as long swirling ridges on the surface.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_07_name;
description: This highly atmosphere-adaptive Species of Bacteria appears as an intricate web pattern. They thrive in proximity to most magma and geyser volcanic activity, especially nitrogen-, methane- or water-based. However, Tela are also found on planetary bodies with no evident Volcanic activity.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_13_name;
description: This Species of Bacteria appears as an intricate web pattern. They show a preference for exclusively water-based volcanic activity.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_05_name;
description: This Species of Bacteria survives on worlds with argon atmospheres and appears as a collection of tight loops on the ground.

name: $codex_ent_bacterial_09_name;
description: This Species of Bacteria is dependent upon oxygen atmospheres and creates random swirling patterns across the ground.

name: $codex_ent_cactoid_01_name;
description: A Species of Cactoida that can reach over three metres in height. They are composed of multiple growths that sprout sealed pods at their peaks, which open up to distribute seeds.

name: $codex_ent_cactoid_02_name;
description: This Species of Cactoida appears as a squat growth with a latticed upper surface, which eventually produces a cluster of seed pods.

name: $codex_ent_cactoid_05_name;
description: This Cactoida Species appears as a swollen five-sided growth, reaching over two metres high and topped with an intersected crown. It is only found in ammonia atmospheres.

name: $codex_ent_cactoid_04_name;
description: This Species of Cactoida, exclusively found in carbon dioxide atmospheres, has a globular base, from which extend vertical cylinders that can reach over four metres. Rounded pods grow in clusters along the cylinders, which break open to scatter seeds.

name: $codex_ent_cactoid_03_name;
description: This Cactoida Species appears as a tall collection of cylinders linked by an undulating membrane and topped with a spiky crown. They often have a spiny life-form attached that is thought to form a symbiotic relationship with the larger organism, although the nature of this is not understood. Vermis is the most heat-tolerant of Cactoida, as is to be expected given its requirement for a Water atmosphere, and far exceeds other Species of the Genus.

name: $codex_ent_clypeus_01_name;
description: A Species of Clypeus that grows a broad, tear-shaped shield to protect the sensitive organism from extreme sunlight. The shield’s ridges help to direct Water droplets down into the soil.

name: $codex_ent_clypeus_02_name;
description: This Clypeus Species produces a curved shield that resembles a large pearl in shape and texture. Up to three central organisms grow within it upon a supporting bed of leaves.

name: $codex_ent_clypeus_03_name;
description: A Clypeus Species that grows an angular shield with a mirrored exterior to protect the spiky organisms.

name: $codex_ent_conchas_02_name;
description: These Concha are found on worlds with nitrogen-based atmospheres. Their rounded rock-like structure splits apart to extend long stalks topped with loops.

name: $codex_ent_conchas_03_name;
description: A concha Species that thrives in atmospheres rich with carbon dioxide. The lip-like upper opening cracks apart to allow a vertical growth of spiky leaves and bright seeds to stretch upward.

name: $codex_ent_conchas_04_name;
description: This Concha Species resembles a ridged, bisected egg until it cracks in half, allowing a thin stalk to sprout from its fleshy insides. This is covered with doughnut-shaped pods that create locations for chemical exchange.

name: $codex_ent_conchas_01_name;
description: A Species of Concha that relies on heat sources to survive. As the bisected growth increases in size, it sprouts a single stalk topped with an array of luminous fronds that facilitate metabolism.

name: $codex_ent_electricae_01_name;
description: A Species of Electricae that extends a tip of four connected loops above the ice, each covered with brightly luminous fronds. This Species is most typically found on planets or their moons in systems containing Stellar Classes: A, White Dwarf or Neutron.

name: $codex_ent_electricae_02_name;
description: This Electricae Species protrudes bioluminescent stalks that radiate out in all directions. There is evidence that this Species has an as yet uncalibrated link with the proximity of nebulae to its host planet.

name: $codex_ent_fonticulus_02_name;
description: These Fonticulua thrive in argon atmospheres, and can reach four metres in height. They feature huge leaf-like structures to capture sunlight for conversion to energy.

name: $codex_ent_fonticulus_06_name;
description: A Fonticulua Species that thrives in methane-based atmospheres, sprouting a cluster of cylindrical tubes directly from the ice.

name: $codex_ent_fonticulus_05_name;
description: A Species of Fonticulua that exists on worlds with oxygen atmospheres. They produce coiling wave-shaped structures which tilt toward sunlight.

name: $codex_ent_fonticulus_04_name;
description: A Fonticulua Species that exists in atmospheres with a heavy concentration of nitrogen. Growing up along the main stalk are bright gem-like pods which can break off and create new colonies.

name: $codex_ent_fonticulus_01_name;
description: A Species of Fonticulua found in atmospheres dominated by neon and appearing as a pyramid-shaped cluster of frilled sections.

name: $codex_ent_fonticulus_03_name;
description: This Fonticulua Species can be found on ice worlds with argon-rich atmospheres. They produce broad hoop-shaped structures to better reflect weak sunlight onto themselves for photosynthesis.

name: $codex_ent_shrubs_02_name;
description: This Frutexa Species has vivid colouration when young that alters as it matures, its upper branches produce lines of small pea-like seed pods.

name: $codex_ent_shrubs_07_name;
description: A Species of Frutexa characterised by its spiky lower branches surrounding a thick central column, which is clotted with spores and with a dark crown.

name: $codex_ent_shrubs_05_name;
description: This Species of Frutexa combines broad branches with long thin stalks, along which grow clusters of lightweight seed pods that are scattered by light winds.

name: $codex_ent_shrubs_01_name;
description: A Species of Frutexa that appears as a bush of leaves with a similar texture to seaweed. Seeds are extended on long stalks and protected by a cage formation until ready to germinate.

name: $codex_ent_shrubs_04_name;
description: A Frutexa Species that gives the appearance of flames, with vivid upright fronds extended from multiple stalks. The fronds are dotted with disc-shaped spores that are distributed by the wind.

name: $codex_ent_shrubs_03_name;
description: This Species of Frutexa has an almost metallic shine to its small leaves. Along its upper branches grow spherical spores, which each have a star-shaped opening to increase germination.

name: $codex_ent_shrubs_06_name;
description: A Frutexa Species that produces clusters of upright intertwining branches, which are crowned with bright seed sacks.

name: $codex_ent_fumerolas_04_name;
description: A Species of Fumerola that can be found near sites of water-based volcanic activity. They appear as small dark clusters with ridged folds that trap heat within.

name: $codex_ent_fumerolas_01_name;
description: A Fumerola Species found near sites of carbon-based volcanism, appearing as a thin upright tube. An inner organism protrudes from an opening at its peak to increase heat absorption.

name: $codex_ent_fumerolas_02_name;
description: This Fumerola Species has a preference for particulate-based volcanism types. They appear as long vertical stalks with smaller fronds that can stretch out.

name: $codex_ent_fumerolas_03_name;
description: This Species of Fumerola prefers nitrogen-based volcanism. They produce an ovoid organism with dotted markings, which sits on top of a thin stalk.

name: $codex_ent_fungoids_03_name;
description: A Fungoida that features clusters of mottled bubble-shaped growths atop a central stalk. These contain spores that can be exposed to the winds to facilitate distribution.

name: $codex_ent_fungoids_04_name;
description: This Fungoida Species resembles an upturned jellyfish, emerging from a solid base buried within the substrate. The exposed part is dominated by fleshy reproductive organisms that shed organic tissue. This tissue can float on the light breeze and form a new organism if it lands in the right location.

name: $codex_ent_fungoids_01_name;
description: This Fungoida Species produces vertical clusters interspersed with spore pods atop thin stalks, allowing them to break off and scatter to reproduce elsewhere.

name: $codex_ent_fungoids_02_name;
description: This Fungoida Species produces vertical clusters interspersed with spore pods atop thin stalks, allowing them to break off and scatter to reproduce elsewhere.

name: $codex_ent_osseus_05_name;
description: An Osseus Species that produces a stacked series of spiral structures up to about three metres in height. These ridged features are upturned to better absorb sunlight for photosynthesis.

name: $codex_ent_osseus_01_name;
description: This Osseus Species can grow to over six metres across. They produce wide ridged frills for metabolic interactions including absorbing sunlight for energy production.

name: $codex_ent_osseus_06_name;
description: A Species of Osseus with a single broad stalk from which extend wide circular structures, with the largest plate capping the top to maximise sunlight absorption.

name: $codex_ent_osseus_03_name;
description: A Species of Osseus that produces coiling spiral structures up to six metres wide. There are ridged folds on their upturned surfaces designed to capture sunlight.

name: $codex_ent_osseus_02_name;
description: An Osseus Species that appears as half-buried discs with radial patterns, which may resemble natural rock formations from a distance. They absorb geothermal energy from below the surface as well as available heat sources above ground.

name: $codex_ent_osseus_04_name;
description: An Osseus Species that grows a single thick stalk from which emerges a wide, broadly circular, pitted endoskeleton. This structure is designed to dramatically increase the surface area to volume of the organism, facilitating chemical capture and chemosynthesis on its catalytically active surface.

name: $codex_ent_recepta_01_name;
description: A Recepta Species that grows a thick latticed structure for protection. A fine translucent membrane stretches between its gaps, allowing sunlight to penetrate and reach the inner organism for photosynthesis.

name: $codex_ent_recepta_03_name;
description: A Recepta Species where the body of the organism is suspended above ground inside a sphere-shaped translucent membrane. This is filled with chemical-rich fluid that both protects the organism and provides the chemical soup needed for metabolism. Chemical exchange is controlled actively through the membrane and passively through the extensive root structure.

name: $codex_ent_recepta_02_name;
description: A Recepta Species that produces a thick lattice of trunks in a deltahedron shape. This grows around and above the globular central organism, and helps to capture, retain and focus geothermal heat for thermosynthesis.

name: $codex_ent_stratum_04_name;
description: A Stratum Species that has a vaguely octopoid shape. The pale semi-translucent upper domes can reveal colourful inner organisms, which contrast with the darker outstretched tentacles. Surprisingly, only Araneamus’ Emerald Variant has ever been recorded. Araneamus is one of the rarer biologicals (only 0.3% of recorded biological population) and it may be that only the Emerald Variant exists, for reasons unknown.

name: $codex_ent_stratum_06_name;
description: A Stratum Species that displays fleshy ovoid shapes that are connected in a narrow pattern across the ground. These are covered with streaks of round photosynthetic cells that absorb sunlight.

name: $codex_ent_stratum_01_name;
description: This Stratum Species appears as a mixture of tight concentric ring patterns and mottled proto-leaves in a mixture of dark hues.

name: $codex_ent_stratum_08_name;
description: This Species of Stratum forms broad interconnected ring structures, which are composed of narrow ridges to capture sunlight.

name: $codex_ent_stratum_03_name;
description: This particular Stratum Species gives the appearance of overlapping rock plateaus, each with narrow bands of colouration.

name: $codex_ent_stratum_05_name;
description: This Species of Stratum appears as a series of unconnected ovoid shapes across the ground, which are the protruding tips of the larger subterranean organism.

name: $codex_ent_stratum_02_name;
description: A Stratum Species that blends thick overlapping vines with irregular growths, with varying colours appearing in bands or streaks.

name: $codex_ent_stratum_07_name;
description: A Stratum Species with a thick rock-like outer shell, covered with an irregular lattice of brighter cells that absorb sunlight for photosynthesis.

name: $codex_ent_tubus_03_name;
description: A Tubus Species that extends pale vertical stalks composed of rigid modules. Colourful fronds frequently appear in the gaps between segments and aid with controlling gaseous exchange.

name: $codex_ent_tubus_05_name;
description: A Tubus Species with narrow pale segments and fronds growing between each module. A wide crown of leaves at the peak hold spores on their undersides, to germinate across a wide area.

name: $codex_ent_tubus_01_name;
description: A Tubus Species formed from hollow vertical cylinders that can reach heights of six metres. Mature specimens are capped with a downturned crown that can distribute seeds on the wind across a wide area.

name: $codex_ent_tubus_04_name;
description: This Tubus Species is composed of squat tubes growing into a vertical spire. The upper pods of mature specimens produce explosive seed pods on their outer skin.

name: $codex_ent_tubus_02_name;
description: This Species of Tubus grows a cluster of hollow stalks composed of rigid segments. Over time these become capped with a growth that flowers and produces seeds.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_08_name;
description: A Tussock Species characterised by leaves with a distinctive striped pattern that are bisected like a snake’s tongue. Mature versions also sprout smaller leaves which produce spores.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_15_name;
description: This Tussock Species is a squat cluster of leaves resembling thick matted hair. From the top of these sprout thick pods that carry a number of round beans.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_11_name;
description: A Tussock Species with leaves that have a thick segmented lower half and a willowy upper half. Mature versions produce separate stalks that carry ovoid organisms clotted with spores.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_05_name;
description: This Species of Tussock has very thin stalks carrying twin sets of seed sacks along their entire length, resembling links on a chain.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_04_name;
description: A Tussock Species with tall sharp reeds reaching about two metres, characterised by narrow markings along their length.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_10_name;
description: This Tussock Species blends thick segmented lower growths with longer, narrower leaves. Mature versions have pale spores along the upper branches.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_03_name;
description: This Tussock Species produces thick intertwined leaves, above which sprout narrow stems crowned with seed pods.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_01_name;
description: A Tussock Species that extends large seed pods on thin stems above a cluster of bright leaves.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_06_name;
description: A Tussock Species with feather-shaped growths surrounding a single segmented stem which when mature is crowned with colourful seeds.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_09_name;
description: A Tussock Species that sprouts tapering leaves, with tips covered with colourful seed pods.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_07_name;
description: This Tussock Species sprouts serrated leaves around thick stalks that produce dark seed pods.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_13_name;
description: This Tussock Species resembles a patch of tough, wiry grasses. Taller stalks carrying disc-shaped seed pods rise above the main organism when mature.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_12_name;
description: A Tussock Species with thin tough leaves marked with dark stripes. From these sprout taller stalks with small leaves, from which seeds are released to the winds.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_02_name;
description: A Tussock Species that blends tough lower stalks with taller willowy reeds, which produce small pale spores.

name: $codex_ent_tussocks_14_name;
description: A Tussock Species with thin reeds clustered around a central stalk, which is eventually crowned with spores.
